planting.note: Plant aerial bulblets 1–2 inches deep in fall or spring in any well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Egyptian walking onion is essentially indestructible and multiplies vigorously with no special care — an excellent permanent addition to the perennial food garden.

directions: Plant aerial bulblets 1–2 inches deep in fall in any well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; Egyptian walking onion is a perennial onion cross that 'walks' itself across the garden by producing clusters of small bulblets at the top of its flowering stalks — when the bulblets become heavy enough, the stalk bends to the ground and the bulblets root where they touch soil, establishing a new cluster a foot away. The entire plant is edible year-round: the underground bulbs are used like small onions, the hollow green tops are used like green onions or chives, and the aerial bulblets are pickled or used as pearl onions. Varieties with particularly fine bulblet clusters and flavor include 'Zebrune' and 'Catawissa'. Essentially zero-maintenance once established — just harvest and leave the rest to multiply. An outstanding permanent edible for the low-maintenance food garden.

planting.note: Sow seed in early spring or fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; the golden cultivar 'Aureum' (Bowles' Golden Grass) seeds true to color and naturalizes readily in appropriate shaded, moist conditions.

directions: Sow seed in early spring or fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; golden wood millet — especially the cultivar 'Aureum' (Bowles' Golden Grass) — is one of the most luminous and valuable shade plants available, producing tufts of graceful, arching bright gold-yellow leaves that seem to glow with internal light in the darkest garden corners. The delicate, open-branched flower panicles appear in spring with golden seeds carried on wispy threads, adding airy texture. A cool-season grass that peaks in spring, goes semi-dormant in summer heat, then revives in fall. It self-sows generously (the golden form seeds true) to naturalize in moist, shaded woodland gardens. Cut back in late summer if it looks tired. Combines beautifully with blue hostas, spring wildflowers, and ferns where its golden light provides striking contrast in deep shade.

planting.note: Direct sow seed in fall in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; spring cress grows naturally in wet woodland springs, seeps, and stream banks and requires consistently moist to wet, cool soil. It self-sows to naturalize in appropriate wet, shaded conditions.

directions: Direct sow in fall in moist to wet, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; spring cress is a native American Cardamine (mustard family) of woodland springs, seeps, and shaded stream banks — one of the earliest edible wild plants of the year, producing rosettes of smooth, edible leaves in late winter with a pleasant, mild watercress-like flavor, followed by masses of small white 4-petaled flowers in early spring. Both leaves and flowers are edible fresh in salads or as a potherb. It grows naturally in the same wet, shaded, cool conditions as watercress and is an excellent native alternative in the shaded water garden or boggy woodland edge. The small bulb-like stem base gives it the common name 'bulbous cress.' A beloved early spring edible in Appalachian foraging tradition. Naturalizes to form showy colonies in appropriate conditions.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist, slightly acidic, deeply humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; never dig trilliums from wild populations — purchase only nursery-propagated plants. The rhizome is easily damaged; plant with care at the same depth as grown.

directions: Plant nursery-grown rhizome divisions in fall in deeply humus-rich, moist, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade — red trillium (T. erectum) is one of the most widespread and variable of all trilliums, producing maroon to dark red (rarely white or pink) three-petaled flowers in mid-spring on erect stalks above a whorl of three broad, mottled leaves. The flower has a distinctive unpleasant odor (resembling carrion) that attracts blow flies and carrion beetles as pollinators. Despite the malodorous flowers, it is a beautiful and ecologically important native wildflower of eastern woodland, associated with rich, mesic forests from Canada to the Appalachians. The seeds are dispersed by ants (a process called myrmecochory) that carry the elaiosome-bearing seeds underground and leave them near established ant colonies. Like all trilliums, red trillium takes 7 years to flower from seed; purchase only nursery-grown, propagated plants — never collect from wild.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in very well-drained, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), sandy, humus-rich, dry to moist soil in partial shade; dwarf iris requires excellent drainage and strongly acidic soil — it will not tolerate clay, wet, or neutral to alkaline conditions.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in very well-drained, strongly acidic, sandy, humus-rich soil in partial shade; dwarf iris is one of the smallest and most charming native irises of the eastern US, producing miniature (2–3 inch) violet-blue flowers with yellow signals on stems only 3–5 inches tall in early spring — often the first iris to bloom, along with hepatica and spring beauty. It grows naturally in dry, acidic, sandy soils of open pine woodlands and dry, rocky woodland edges from Virginia to Georgia, in conditions very different from the moist requirements of most native irises. The leaves are narrow and grasslike. It spreads slowly by slender rhizomes to form a low colony. Excellent for naturalizing in acidic, dry-shaded rock gardens or under pines in the Southeast — one of the few dwarf irises suited to zone 8–9 conditions.
